fuzzy_prd:
We need a small plugin-style hook for an existing Markdown rendering engine already in use by our editor. Some documents are regular notes, but some should render as slide decks. The key challenge is that the engine is shared, so we cannot swap it out globally — the hook must leave normal documents completely untouched and only activate the slide-deck behavior when a document explicitly requests it through a configuration flag at the very top of the file.

When slide mode is on, the output HTML must include a uniquely identifiable container element and an injected stylesheet so the preview layer can detect whether it is looking at a slide or a regular document. The slide renderer should also handle emoji displayed as images rather than Unicode characters, lay out math formulas with a special fitting treatment, and syntax-highlight code blocks using the slide engine's own highlighter. For diagram blocks that the highlighter does not understand, the content should be wrapped for a downstream diagramming tool. If the language tag is entirely unknown, the code block should still preserve that label so consumers can identify it.

Existing third-party plugins for emoji and math that are already registered on the engine must continue to work correctly in non-slide mode. In slide mode, the hook should silently take over those rendering paths without the caller having to deregister the original plugins.

Finally, the library must expose a stable entry point — referenced in the VS Code extension manifest — so that the host can obtain the hook through a single standard call rather than importing internals directly.

trigger_keywords: when does slide rendering turn on
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Slide mode is activated per-render by inspecting the document's leading front-matter block. The front-matter must match the regex /^---\s*([^]*)?(?:-{3}|\.{3})\s*/ and within its body the directive `marp: true` must appear as a standalone line (matched by /^marp\s*:\s*true\s*$/m). If either condition is not met, rendering proceeds as plain Markdown and no slide markers are injected.

trigger_keywords: how is the slide container identified
what HTML element wraps the slide
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The Marp instance is constructed with `container: { tag: 'div', id: 'marp-vscode' }`. The slide container in the rendered HTML is therefore `<div id="marp-vscode">`. The injected stylesheet element is `<style id="marp-vscode-style">...</style>` and is prepended to the token stream via a custom core rule named `marp_vscode_style`.

trigger_keywords: how does emoji rendering work in slide mode
what happens to the emoji plugin when marp is active
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the hook detects that a third-party emoji renderer rule (`renderer.rules.emoji`) exists on the engine, it wraps it inside a new function. The wrapper checks the per-instance `marpVscodeEnabled` symbol flag: if true it delegates to `renderer.rules.marp_emoji` (the slide engine's own emoji rule, which emits `data-marp-twemoji`); otherwise it calls the original emoji rule. This override is installed lazily in the `marp_vscode_postprocess` core rule and is applied only once.

trigger_keywords: how are math block rules disabled in slide mode
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: To avoid collision with third-party math plugins such as `@neilsustc/markdown-it-katex`, the `marp_vscode_toggle` core rule explicitly enables or disables the markdown-it block and inline math rules: when `marp: true` is detected, `md.block.ruler.disable('math_block', true)` and `md.inline.ruler.disable('math_inline', true)` are called; when marp is off, both are re-enabled with `true` (silent mode). This hands math parsing back to the external plugin when slide mode is inactive.

trigger_keywords: how is the base highlighter superseded
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
The original `md.options.highlight` function is captured before being replaced. A new `marpHighlighter` function is installed via `md.set({ highlight: ... })`. When `md[marpVscodeEnabled]` is true it calls `marp.highlighter(code, lang)` first; if that returns a non-empty string it is used. If the language is `mermaid` (case-insensitive) it returns `
${md.utils.escapeHtml(code)}
`. For any other unrecognized language it returns an empty string, which causes markdown-it to fall back to its default code rendering that preserves the language class as `language-`. When marp is disabled, the original `highlight` function is called unchanged.

trigger_keywords: how to get the extension hook from the library
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The library exports `activate` as a named export: `export const activate = () => ({ extendMarkdownIt })`. It returns a plain object with a single property `extendMarkdownIt` pointing to the hook function. The adapter confirms the hook is usable by checking `typeof api.extendMarkdownIt === 'function'` and emits `extend_hook=present` before probing any signals.

trigger_keywords: what substrings are checked for each signal
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The PROBES mapping used by the dispatcher is: container -> `<div id="marp-vscode">`, style -> `<style id="marp-vscode-style">`, svg -> `svg`, img -> `img`, twemoji -> `data-marp-twemoji`, katex -> `<span class="katex">`, fitting_math -> `data-marp-fitting-math`, hljs -> `hljs`, base_highlight -> `baseHighlight`, mermaid -> `<div class="mermaid">`, unknown_lang -> `<code class="language-unknownlang">`. Each signal is reported as `<name>=present` if the substring is found anywhere in the rendered HTML, otherwise `<name>=absent`, one per line in the order listed in the `signals` array, each line terminated with a newline.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: a configuration flag at the very top of the file
context_pointers.actual_meaning: A YAML front-matter block at the start of the Markdown source containing the line `marp: true`, detected by regex /^---\s*([^]*)?(?:-{3}|\.{3})\s*/ combined with /^marp\s*:\s*true\s*$/m

context_pointers.vague_reference: a uniquely identifiable container element and an injected stylesheet
context_pointers.actual_meaning: `<div id="marp-vscode">` (the Marp container configured with `{ tag: 'div', id: 'marp-vscode' }`) and `<style id="marp-vscode-style">` (prepended by the `marp_vscode_style` core rule)

context_pointers.vague_reference: a special fitting treatment for math formulas
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Marp core engine wraps math formulas with `data-marp-fitting-math` attributes when slide mode is active; the third-party math block/inline rules are disabled to prevent double-rendering

context_pointers.vague_reference: wrapped for a downstream diagramming tool
context_pointers.actual_meaning:
Fenced blocks tagged `mermaid` are rendered as `
${md.utils.escapeHtml(code)}
` in the custom highlighter, making the content available to a downstream Mermaid.js plugin

context_pointers.vague_reference: a stable entry point referenced in the VS Code extension manifest
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `activate` exported function in `src/extension.ts`, which VS Code calls as the extension activation entry point and which returns `{ extendMarkdownIt }` for the host to use

oracle_data.fallback_response: The question does not match any documented constraint for this task. Please refer to the front-matter detection logic, the PROBES signal mapping, the emoji/math/highlighter override behavior, or the `activate` entry point signature described in the hidden constraints.

feature1_frontmatter_activation.json.description: An extensible Markdown engine is wrapped by a slide-extension hook. The hook only activates slide rendering when the document begins with a front-matter block that turns the slide mode on (a `marp: true` directive); otherwise the document is rendered as ordinary Markdown. Activation is observable through several wire-format markers in the produced HTML: a slide container element with id `marp-vscode`, an injected style element with id `marp-vscode-style`, and the presence of inline `svg` and `img` output produced by the slide renderer. The input supplies the raw Markdown source and the list of markers to probe; each probed marker is reported as `present` or `absent`. When slide mode is off, none of the markers appear; when on, all of them appear.

feature2_emoji_override.json.description: A third-party emoji plugin is registered on the Markdown engine before the slide-extension hook is applied. When slide mode is active, the hook overrides the emoji renderer so that emoji are rendered through the slide engine's own emoji renderer, which emits a distinguishing wire-format marker attribute (`data-marp-twemoji`) on the output. When slide mode is inactive, the original emoji plugin's renderer is left untouched and that marker does not appear. The input supplies the Markdown source (an emoji shortcode) and registers the emoji plugin; output reports whether the slide engine's emoji marker is present.

feature3_math_rendering.json.description: A third-party math plugin (KaTeX-based) is registered on the Markdown engine before the slide-extension hook is applied. In both modes the math plugin renders formulas, so the KaTeX wire-format marker (`<span class="katex">`) is always present. The slide engine additionally applies its own fitting-math treatment to formulas, which is observable through a distinguishing marker attribute (`data-marp-fitting-math`). That fitting-math marker appears only when slide mode is active; in plain Markdown mode the formulas are rendered by the math plugin alone, without the fitting-math marker. The input supplies the Markdown source containing inline and block math and registers the math plugin; output reports the two markers.

feature4_1_highlighter_selection.json.description: The Markdown engine is constructed with a base syntax highlighter (a caller-supplied highlight function that emits a recognizable sentinel string `baseHighlight`). The slide-extension hook installs its own highlighter that supersedes the base one only while slide mode is active. When slide mode is OFF, a fenced code block is highlighted by the base highlighter, so its sentinel appears and the slide engine's highlight markup (the `hljs` class) does not. When slide mode is ON, the slide engine's highlighter handles the code block instead: the slide highlight markup (`hljs`) appears and the base highlighter's sentinel is absent. The input supplies the Markdown source (a fenced code block in a known language), requests installation of the base highlighter, and probes both markers.

feature4_2_mermaid_block.json.description: While slide mode is active, the slide engine's highlighter has no built-in grammar for the `mermaid` fenced-code language. Instead of failing, a fenced code block tagged as `mermaid` is wrapped in a diagram container element (`<div class="mermaid">`) whose body is the HTML-escaped source code, so a downstream diagram plugin can render it. The input supplies Markdown containing a `mermaid` fenced block; output reports whether the diagram container marker is present.

feature4_3_unknown_language.json.description: While slide mode is active, a fenced code block tagged with an unrecognized language (one the slide highlighter cannot highlight and which is not a special-cased diagram language) is passed through as a plain code element that preserves the language as a class on the `<code>` element (`<code class="language-unknownlang">`). This keeps the original language label available to consumers even when no highlighting is applied. The input supplies Markdown containing a fenced block in an unknown language; output reports whether the language-preserving code marker is present.

feature5_activation_entry.json.description: The library exposes an activation entry point that returns an object whose member is the Markdown slide-extension hook. The hook obtained from the activation entry must be a usable function: applying it to a Markdown engine and rendering a slide-mode document produces the slide output markers (the container element and the injected style element). The input requests the activation entry and supplies a slide-mode Markdown document plus the markers to probe; output first reports that the extension hook is present, then reports each requested marker as observed after rendering through the hook.
